title: Day 12 Vocabulary — Use of Wanted (100 Words)

words.simpleMeaning: to have a desire for something

sentences.daily: I want to drink water.
sentences.office: We want to finalize this deal.
sentences.interview: I want to grow professionally.
sentences.business: Clients want fast delivery.
sentences.formal: He wants to request a meeting.
sentences.informal: Do you want some food?

words.usageNote: 'Want' is present; 'wanted' is past. Stative verb — avoid continuous form in formal use.

words.simpleMeaning: to desire something, often unreal or unlikely

sentences.daily: I wish I had more time.
sentences.office: I wish the meeting were shorter.
sentences.interview: I wish to contribute to your team.
sentences.business: We wish you continued success.
sentences.formal: He wishes to withdraw his application.
sentences.informal: I wish you were here.

words.usageNote: 'Wish' + past tense expresses unreal desire. 'Wished' = past form, similar to 'wanted'.

words.simpleMeaning: a strong wish or feeling of wanting something

sentences.daily: She desired a peaceful life.
sentences.office: He desired a senior position.
sentences.interview: I desire to excel in this role.
sentences.business: The company desired rapid growth.
sentences.formal: She desired a formal response.
sentences.informal: He desired more attention.

words.usageNote: 'Desire' is more formal and intense than 'want'. Often used in writing.

words.simpleMeaning: to want something to happen and believe it might

sentences.daily: I hoped for good weather.
sentences.office: We hoped to close the deal by Friday.
sentences.interview: I hope to join your team soon.
sentences.business: They hoped for higher profits.
sentences.formal: He hoped to receive a positive reply.
sentences.informal: I hoped you would come.

words.usageNote: 'Hope' involves belief it might happen. 'Hoped' is past form.

words.simpleMeaning: to imagine or wish for something ideal

sentences.daily: I dreamed of visiting Paris.
sentences.office: She dreamed of running her own firm.
sentences.interview: I have always dreamed of this opportunity.
sentences.business: He dreamed of building a successful brand.
sentences.formal: She dreamed of excelling in academia.
sentences.informal: We dreamed of going on a road trip.

words.usageNote: Can also use 'dreamt' (British English). Similar to 'wanted to' in meaning.

words.simpleMeaning: to think that something will happen

sentences.daily: I expected a call from him.
sentences.office: We expected the report by Monday.
sentences.interview: I expected a challenging role.
sentences.business: Clients expected timely delivery.
sentences.formal: She expected a formal acknowledgement.
sentences.informal: I expected you earlier!

words.usageNote: 'Expected' = past tense. 'Expect' is different from 'want' — it implies belief, not just desire.

words.simpleMeaning: to decide and arrange what you will do

sentences.daily: I planned to wake up early.
sentences.office: She planned to present her findings.
sentences.interview: I planned this career path carefully.
sentences.business: We planned to launch in Q3.
sentences.formal: He planned to submit the proposal.
sentences.informal: We planned to hang out this weekend.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to' + 'planned to' often go together: 'I wanted to but hadn't planned to.'

words.simpleMeaning: to make a choice about something

sentences.daily: I decided to quit social media.
sentences.office: She decided to delegate the task.
sentences.interview: I decided to switch careers.
sentences.business: The board decided to restructure.
sentences.formal: He decided to escalate the matter.
sentences.informal: We decided to skip the party.

words.usageNote: 'Decided' and 'wanted to' often appear together: 'I wanted to do it but decided not to.'

words.simpleMeaning: to make an attempt to do something

sentences.daily: I tried to fix it myself.
sentences.office: She tried to meet the deadline.
sentences.interview: I tried every opportunity to grow.
sentences.business: We tried to retain the client.
sentences.formal: He tried to resolve the conflict.
sentences.informal: I tried calling you three times.

words.usageNote: 'Tried to' = past attempt. Often pairs with 'wanted to': 'I wanted to do it and tried.'

words.simpleMeaning: to require something; to lack something necessary

sentences.daily: I needed more time.
sentences.office: We needed more staff for the project.
sentences.interview: I needed a growth-oriented environment.
sentences.business: The company needed a stronger strategy.
sentences.formal: He needed additional documentation.
sentences.informal: I just needed a break.

words.usageNote: 'Needed' vs 'wanted' — 'needed' is necessity, 'wanted' is desire. Both are past tense.

words.simpleMeaning: to say no to something; to be unwilling to do it

sentences.daily: He refused to eat vegetables.
sentences.office: She refused to sign the contract.
sentences.interview: I never refuse a learning opportunity.
sentences.business: The client refused the revised offer.
sentences.formal: He refused to comment on the matter.
sentences.informal: She refused to come to the party.

words.usageNote: 'Refused to' = past refusal. Opposite in meaning to 'wanted to'.

words.simpleMeaning: to have the same opinion; to say yes

sentences.daily: I agreed to help her.
sentences.office: We agreed to the new terms.
sentences.interview: I agreed with the interviewer's approach.
sentences.business: Both parties agreed on the price.
sentences.formal: He agreed to sign the MOU.
sentences.informal: She finally agreed to come.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to agree' = desired to say yes. Common in professional settings.

words.simpleMeaning: to request something or seek information

sentences.daily: I wanted to ask her for help.
sentences.office: She asked for a project extension.
sentences.interview: I wanted to ask about the work culture.
sentences.business: He asked for a better deal.
sentences.formal: She asked to be excused from the meeting.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to ask if you are free.

words.usageNote: 'Asked' = past. 'Wanted to ask' is very common polite phrase.

words.simpleMeaning: to communicate information to someone

sentences.daily: I wanted to tell you something important.
sentences.office: She told the manager about the issue.
sentences.interview: I wanted to tell you about my achievements.
sentences.business: He told the client about the delay.
sentences.formal: She wanted to tell the board about the results.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to tell you — you did great!

words.usageNote: Irregular: tell → told → told. 'Wanted to tell' is a very natural spoken phrase.

words.simpleMeaning: to come together with someone

sentences.daily: I wanted to meet him at the park.
sentences.office: She wanted to meet the new client.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to meet people in this industry.
sentences.business: We wanted to meet before the deal closed.
sentences.formal: He wanted to meet the CEO personally.
sentences.informal: I wanted to meet you sooner.

words.usageNote: Irregular: meet → met → met. 'Wanted to meet' is very common in both social and professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to go to see a person, place or thing

sentences.daily: I wanted to visit my grandparents.
sentences.office: We wanted to visit the client's office.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to visit this company.
sentences.business: The investor wanted to visit the factory.
sentences.formal: She wanted to visit the overseas branch.
sentences.informal: He wanted to visit us last weekend.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to visit' = past desire to go somewhere. Very useful in travel and family contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to gain knowledge or a skill

sentences.daily: I wanted to learn guitar.
sentences.office: She wanted to learn a new software.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to learn from the best.
sentences.business: He wanted to learn digital marketing.
sentences.formal: She wanted to learn best practices.
sentences.informal: I wanted to learn cooking from my mother.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to learn' = past desire to acquire skill. Very common in interviews and academic contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to successfully reach a goal or standard

sentences.daily: I wanted to achieve my fitness goal.
sentences.office: She wanted to achieve the sales target.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to achieve something meaningful.
sentences.business: We wanted to achieve market leadership.
sentences.formal: He wanted to achieve regulatory compliance.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to achieve something this year.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to achieve' is a powerful phrase in interviews and goal-setting discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: to make or become different

sentences.daily: I wanted to change my daily routine.
sentences.office: She wanted to change the project approach.
sentences.interview: I wanted to change my career path.
sentences.business: He wanted to change the pricing model.
sentences.formal: The board wanted to change the policy.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to change the plan.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to change' expresses past desire for transformation, very useful in interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something better

sentences.daily: I wanted to improve my English.
sentences.office: She wanted to improve team performance.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to improve myself.
sentences.business: We wanted to improve customer satisfaction.
sentences.formal: He wanted to improve the documentation.
sentences.informal: I wanted to improve my cooking.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to improve' is extremely common in self-development, interview, and professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to assist someone or make things easier

sentences.daily: I wanted to help my brother.
sentences.office: She wanted to help the new employee.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to help others grow.
sentences.business: He wanted to help the client solve the issue.
sentences.formal: She wanted to help with the event arrangements.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to help you.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to help' is a natural, kind expression used across all contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to become a member or part of a group

sentences.daily: I wanted to join the yoga class.
sentences.office: She wanted to join the core team.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to join this organization.
sentences.business: He wanted to join the industry association.
sentences.formal: She wanted to join the advisory board.
sentences.informal: I wanted to join you all for lunch.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to join' is extremely common in interview answers and social contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to begin an activity or journey

sentences.daily: I wanted to start reading novels.
sentences.office: She wanted to start a new initiative.
sentences.interview: I wanted to start my career here.
sentences.business: He wanted to start his own business.
sentences.formal: She wanted to start the meeting on time.
sentences.informal: I wanted to start the day fresh.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to start' is very common in goal-setting, interviews, and business discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: to complete something

sentences.daily: I wanted to finish my homework early.
sentences.office: She wanted to finish the report by noon.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to finish what I started.
sentences.business: We wanted to finish the project on time.
sentences.formal: He wanted to finish the documentation.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to finish the game.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to finish' shows past desire for task completion. Very useful in professional conversations.

words.simpleMeaning: to go from one place to another

sentences.daily: I wanted to travel to Shimla.
sentences.office: She wanted to travel to the client site.
sentences.interview: I have always wanted to travel and experience cultures.
sentences.business: He wanted to travel for the product launch.
sentences.formal: She wanted to travel to the headquarters.
sentences.informal: We wanted to travel together this summer.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to travel' expresses past desire for exploration. Very common in informal and formal contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to talk; to use words to communicate

sentences.daily: I wanted to speak to my teacher.
sentences.office: She wanted to speak at the conference.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to speak fluent English.
sentences.business: He wanted to speak to the CEO directly.
sentences.formal: She wanted to speak at the board meeting.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to speak my mind.

words.usageNote: Irregular: speak → spoke → spoken. 'Wanted to speak' is very natural in daily and professional life.

words.simpleMeaning: to form words or text

sentences.daily: I wanted to write a letter.
sentences.office: She wanted to write the project summary.
sentences.interview: I wanted to write about my experience.
sentences.business: He wanted to write a business proposal.
sentences.formal: She wanted to write a formal complaint.
sentences.informal: I wanted to write a poem for you.

words.usageNote: Irregular: write → wrote → written. 'Wanted to write' is common in creative and professional settings.

words.simpleMeaning: to look at and understand written words

sentences.daily: I wanted to read that novel.
sentences.office: She wanted to read the full report.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to read widely.
sentences.business: He wanted to read the contract carefully.
sentences.formal: She wanted to read the policy document.
sentences.informal: I wanted to read the message but forgot.

words.usageNote: Interesting: 'read' (present) /riːd/ vs 'read' (past) /red/ — same spelling, different pronunciation.

words.simpleMeaning: to get something by paying money

sentences.daily: I wanted to buy new shoes.
sentences.office: She wanted to buy new equipment.
sentences.interview: I wanted to buy books to prepare.
sentences.business: He wanted to buy the startup.
sentences.formal: She wanted to buy the office space.
sentences.informal: I wanted to buy a gift for you.

words.usageNote: Irregular: buy → bought → bought. 'Wanted to buy' is common in daily life and business.

words.simpleMeaning: to exchange for money

sentences.daily: I wanted to sell my old phone.
sentences.office: She wanted to sell more units this quarter.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to sell ideas, not just products.
sentences.business: He wanted to sell the company to investors.
sentences.formal: She wanted to sell the property.
sentences.informal: I wanted to sell my old bike.

words.usageNote: Irregular: sell → sold → sold. 'Wanted to sell' is key in sales, business, and commerce contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to hand over something to someone

sentences.daily: I wanted to give him a surprise.
sentences.office: She wanted to give feedback to her team.
sentences.interview: I wanted to give my best.
sentences.business: He wanted to give a discount.
sentences.formal: She wanted to give a formal presentation.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to give you a hug.

words.usageNote: Irregular: give → gave → given. 'Wanted to give' shows past generosity or intention.

words.simpleMeaning: to pick up or carry something

sentences.daily: I wanted to take a break.
sentences.office: She wanted to take charge of the project.
sentences.interview: I wanted to take on more responsibility.
sentences.business: He wanted to take the company public.
sentences.formal: She wanted to take formal leave.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to take a nap.

words.usageNote: Irregular: take → took → taken. Many phrasal verbs: take charge, take a break, take over.

words.simpleMeaning: to have information about something

sentences.daily: I wanted to know her name.
sentences.office: She wanted to know the project status.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to know more about this field.
sentences.business: He wanted to know the market trend.
sentences.formal: She wanted to know the audit results.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to know if you were okay.

words.usageNote: Irregular: know → knew → known. Stative verb — avoid continuous form. 'Wanted to know' is very natural.

words.simpleMeaning: to know the meaning of something

sentences.daily: I wanted to understand the recipe.
sentences.office: She wanted to understand the new system.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to understand the business deeply.
sentences.business: He wanted to understand the client's need.
sentences.formal: She wanted to understand the legal terms.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to understand your point.

words.usageNote: Irregular: understand → understood → understood. 'Wanted to understand' shows intellectual curiosity.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something clear by describing it

sentences.daily: I wanted to explain my side.
sentences.office: She wanted to explain the new process.
sentences.interview: I wanted to explain my experience clearly.
sentences.business: He wanted to explain the pricing model.
sentences.formal: She wanted to explain the decision rationale.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to explain what happened.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to explain' is very common in both apologies and professional discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: to say sorry for something

sentences.daily: I wanted to apologize to my friend.
sentences.office: She wanted to apologize for the delay.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to apologize and learn from mistakes.
sentences.business: He wanted to apologize for the miscommunication.
sentences.formal: She wanted to apologize formally in writing.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to apologize for being rude.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to apologize' is a very polite and common expression in daily and professional life.

words.simpleMeaning: to do something special for a happy event

sentences.daily: I wanted to celebrate my birthday with family.
sentences.office: She wanted to celebrate the team's success.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to celebrate milestones.
sentences.business: He wanted to celebrate closing the deal.
sentences.formal: She wanted to celebrate the company anniversary.
sentences.informal: We wanted to celebrate all night!

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to celebrate' expresses past desire for joyous occasion.

words.simpleMeaning: to stop working and relax

sentences.daily: I wanted to rest after a long day.
sentences.office: She wanted to rest during the lunch break.
sentences.interview: I wanted rest, which is why I resigned.
sentences.business: He wanted to rest before the big pitch.
sentences.formal: She wanted to rest before the ceremony.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to rest for 10 minutes.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to rest' is very natural in daily conversations about tiredness.

words.simpleMeaning: to change position or place

sentences.daily: I wanted to move to a bigger house.
sentences.office: She wanted to move to the new office.
sentences.interview: I wanted to move to a growth-oriented company.
sentences.business: He wanted to move the business online.
sentences.formal: She wanted to move the meeting to Thursday.
sentences.informal: I wanted to move out of my hometown.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to move' — both literal (location) and figurative (career, life changes).

words.simpleMeaning: to give a part of something to others

sentences.daily: I wanted to share my food with him.
sentences.office: She wanted to share the data with the team.
sentences.interview: I wanted to share my ideas openly.
sentences.business: He wanted to share the profits fairly.
sentences.formal: She wanted to share the findings with stakeholders.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to share something funny.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to share' is very natural in both social and professional settings.

words.simpleMeaning: to give help or encouragement to someone

sentences.daily: I wanted to support my parents.
sentences.office: She wanted to support the junior team members.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to support organizational growth.
sentences.business: He wanted to support the new initiative.
sentences.formal: She wanted to support the CSR program.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to support you.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to support' is important in interview and volunteer contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to keep someone or something safe from harm

sentences.daily: I wanted to protect my family.
sentences.office: She wanted to protect the company's data.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to protect people's interests.
sentences.business: He wanted to protect the brand reputation.
sentences.formal: She wanted to protect confidential information.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to protect you.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to protect' is common in both family and corporate discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: to stay away from someone or something

sentences.daily: I wanted to avoid traffic.
sentences.office: She wanted to avoid conflict.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to avoid negativity.
sentences.business: He wanted to avoid unnecessary costs.
sentences.formal: She wanted to avoid legal complications.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to avoid drama.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to avoid' — followed by gerund (ing) or noun. Common in daily speech.

words.simpleMeaning: to receive money for work done

sentences.daily: I wanted to earn more money.
sentences.office: She wanted to earn a better salary.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to earn through my skills.
sentences.business: He wanted to earn customer trust.
sentences.formal: She wanted to earn recognition in the industry.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to earn enough for the trip.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to earn' is very common in career and finance discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: to keep something safe or to keep money for later

sentences.daily: I wanted to save money for a vacation.
sentences.office: She wanted to save time on paperwork.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to save people from inefficiency.
sentences.business: He wanted to save the company from losses.
sentences.formal: She wanted to save the document before closing.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to save up for a bike.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to save' — both money (finance) and rescuing someone (literal). Very versatile.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something smaller in size or amount

sentences.daily: I wanted to reduce sugar in my diet.
sentences.office: She wanted to reduce the project timeline.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to reduce waste in processes.
sentences.business: He wanted to reduce operational costs.
sentences.formal: She wanted to reduce overhead expenditure.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to reduce stress.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to reduce' is common in health, business, and efficiency discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: to concentrate on something

sentences.daily: I wanted to focus on my studies.
sentences.office: She wanted to focus on key priorities.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to focus on impact.
sentences.business: He wanted to focus on core products.
sentences.formal: She wanted to focus on strategic planning.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to focus and get it done.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to focus' is very common in academic, personal development, and professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to increase in size, knowledge or ability

sentences.daily: I wanted to grow as a person.
sentences.office: She wanted to grow within the company.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to grow in a challenging environment.
sentences.business: He wanted to grow the brand internationally.
sentences.formal: She wanted to grow the client portfolio.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to grow and improve.

words.usageNote: Irregular: grow → grew → grown. 'Wanted to grow' is an extremely common interview phrase.

words.simpleMeaning: to construct or develop something

sentences.daily: I wanted to build a strong habit.
sentences.office: She wanted to build a better process.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to build something from scratch.
sentences.business: He wanted to build a scalable business.
sentences.formal: She wanted to build a strategic alliance.
sentences.informal: I wanted to build a treehouse.

words.usageNote: Irregular: build → built → built. 'Wanted to build' is common in entrepreneurship and interview contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to achieve a desired aim or result

sentences.daily: I always wanted to succeed in life.
sentences.office: She wanted to succeed in her new role.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to succeed in a competitive environment.
sentences.business: He wanted to succeed in international markets.
sentences.formal: She wanted to succeed in the regulatory process.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to succeed at something I loved.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to succeed' is extremely powerful in interviews and motivational contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: a chance to do something or a favorable situation

sentences.daily: I wanted this opportunity to prove myself.
sentences.office: She wanted the opportunity to lead.
sentences.interview: I always wanted an opportunity like this.
sentences.business: He wanted the opportunity to pitch his idea.
sentences.formal: She wanted the opportunity to address concerns.
sentences.informal: I just wanted one opportunity.

words.usageNote: 'Opportunity' is a noun. 'Wanted + noun' — no 'to' needed. Very common in interview answers.

words.simpleMeaning: the achievement of a desired goal

sentences.daily: I always wanted success in my career.
sentences.office: She wanted success for her team.
sentences.interview: I wanted success through hard work.
sentences.business: He wanted success in the global market.
sentences.formal: She wanted success in the project.
sentences.informal: We just wanted success together.

words.usageNote: 'Success' is a noun. 'Wanted success' = past desire for achievement.

words.simpleMeaning: the power to act without restriction

sentences.daily: I wanted freedom to make my own choices.
sentences.office: She wanted freedom to innovate.
sentences.interview: I wanted freedom and ownership in my role.
sentences.business: He wanted freedom from micro-management.
sentences.formal: She wanted freedom of expression.
sentences.informal: I just wanted freedom.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted freedom' — very natural in personal, career, and political contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: a state of calm and freedom from disturbance

sentences.daily: I wanted peace after the argument.
sentences.office: She wanted peace in the team.
sentences.interview: I wanted a peaceful work environment.
sentences.business: He wanted peace with the competitor.
sentences.formal: She wanted peace in the negotiations.
sentences.informal: I just wanted some peace and quiet.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted peace' — very common in both personal and professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: fair treatment and behavior

sentences.daily: She wanted justice for her family.
sentences.office: He wanted justice in the workplace.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to work for justice.
sentences.business: The firm wanted justice in the dispute.
sentences.formal: She wanted justice through legal channels.
sentences.informal: They just wanted justice.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted justice' is powerful in legal and social discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: notice or interest given to someone or something

sentences.daily: The child wanted attention.
sentences.office: She wanted attention on the key issue.
sentences.interview: I wanted to draw attention to my strengths.
sentences.business: He wanted attention for the product launch.
sentences.formal: She wanted attention to detail.
sentences.informal: He just wanted attention.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted attention' is common in social and professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to admire someone for their qualities

sentences.daily: I always wanted respect from my peers.
sentences.office: She wanted respect in the workplace.
sentences.interview: I wanted to earn respect through performance.
sentences.business: He wanted respect from his competitors.
sentences.formal: She wanted her views to be respected.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to be respected.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted respect' or 'wanted to be respected' — both correct and natural.

words.simpleMeaning: advancement to a higher position at work

sentences.daily: He wanted a promotion badly.
sentences.office: She wanted the promotion she deserved.
sentences.interview: I always wanted a promotion based on merit.
sentences.business: He wanted promotion of his brand.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal promotion letter.
sentences.informal: He just wanted that promotion!

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a promotion' is extremely common in professional and interview contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: money paid regularly to an employee

sentences.daily: I wanted a better salary.
sentences.office: She wanted her salary hike on time.
sentences.interview: I wanted a salary that matched my skills.
sentences.business: He wanted competitive salary packages.
sentences.formal: She wanted a salary revision formally.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a decent salary.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a salary' — very common in job interview and career contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: belief in one's own abilities

sentences.daily: I always wanted more confidence.
sentences.office: She wanted the confidence to speak up.
sentences.interview: I wanted confidence in my answers.
sentences.business: He wanted confidence in his product.
sentences.formal: She wanted confidence before the presentation.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to feel confident.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted confidence' or 'wanted to feel confident' — both natural. Common in self-development contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: facts and information acquired through experience

sentences.daily: I always wanted more knowledge.
sentences.office: She wanted knowledge of the new system.
sentences.interview: I wanted knowledge of industry trends.
sentences.business: He wanted knowledge of competitor strategies.
sentences.formal: She wanted in-depth knowledge of the subject.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to gain more knowledge.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted knowledge' = past desire to learn. Very common in academic and professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: knowledge or skill gained over time

sentences.daily: I wanted experience in cooking.
sentences.office: She wanted experience in project management.
sentences.interview: I always wanted experience in this domain.
sentences.business: He wanted experience of international deals.
sentences.formal: She wanted experience in research.
sentences.informal: I just wanted some real-world experience.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted experience' is extremely common in interview answers about career choices.

words.simpleMeaning: the measured or measurable period

sentences.daily: I wanted more time to think.
sentences.office: She wanted time to review the document.
sentences.interview: I wanted time to grow in my previous role.
sentences.business: He wanted time to close the deal.
sentences.formal: She wanted time to prepare the proposal.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a little more time.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted time' or 'wanted more time' — extremely common in daily and professional speech.

words.simpleMeaning: to make or become different

sentences.daily: I wanted a change in my routine.
sentences.office: She wanted change in the team culture.
sentences.interview: I wanted a change that challenged me.
sentences.business: He wanted change in the business model.
sentences.formal: She wanted a change in policy.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a change.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a change' is very common in daily life, career switching, and lifestyle discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: a response to a question or solution to a problem

sentences.daily: I wanted an answer quickly.
sentences.office: She wanted an answer before the meeting.
sentences.interview: I wanted honest answers to my questions.
sentences.business: He wanted a clear answer from the vendor.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal written answer.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a straight answer.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted an answer' is natural in all situations — daily, professional, legal.

words.simpleMeaning: official approval or consent to do something

sentences.daily: I wanted permission to go out.
sentences.office: She wanted permission to work from home.
sentences.interview: I wanted permission to be innovative.
sentences.business: He wanted permission to proceed.
sentences.formal: She wanted written permission.
sentences.informal: I just wanted permission to try.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted permission' is a very natural phrase for social, school, office, and legal contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: facts or knowledge provided about something

sentences.daily: I wanted information about the train.
sentences.office: She wanted more information from the client.
sentences.interview: I wanted information about the company culture.
sentences.business: He wanted detailed information before signing.
sentences.formal: She wanted information in writing.
sentences.informal: I just wanted some information.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted information' is extremely common in daily life, business, and professional settings.

words.simpleMeaning: assistance or support given to someone

sentences.daily: I wanted some help with cooking.
sentences.office: She wanted help from her colleague.
sentences.interview: I never wanted help — I preferred to solve problems myself.
sentences.business: He wanted help to scale the business.
sentences.formal: She wanted help with the proposal.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a little help.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted help' (noun) vs 'wanted to help' (verb) — both correct, different meanings.

words.simpleMeaning: a short rest from work or activity

sentences.daily: I wanted a break after working all morning.
sentences.office: She wanted a break from the project.
sentences.interview: I wanted a break before starting my new job.
sentences.business: He wanted a break to rethink the strategy.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal leave as a break.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a break!

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a break' is very natural in daily life, office, and wellness conversations.

words.simpleMeaning: guidance or recommendation about what to do

sentences.daily: I wanted advice from my parents.
sentences.office: She wanted advice from the senior manager.
sentences.interview: I always wanted advice on career growth.
sentences.business: He wanted advice on the new deal.
sentences.formal: She wanted formal legal advice.
sentences.informal: I just wanted your advice.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted advice' — noun. Compare: 'wanted to advise' — verb. Common in all contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: an answer to a problem or difficult situation

sentences.daily: I wanted a quick solution.
sentences.office: She wanted a long-term solution.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to find solutions, not excuses.
sentences.business: He wanted a cost-effective solution.
sentences.formal: She wanted a documented solution.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a simple solution.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a solution' is very common in problem-solving, business, and daily life.

words.simpleMeaning: the outcome of an action or process

sentences.daily: I wanted good results in my exam.
sentences.office: She wanted measurable results.
sentences.interview: I always wanted results-driven work.
sentences.business: He wanted results in 30 days.
sentences.formal: She wanted documented results.
sentences.informal: I just wanted results, not excuses.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted results' is very common in business, academics, and performance discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: a difficult task that tests your ability

sentences.daily: I always wanted a new challenge.
sentences.office: She wanted to challenge herself.
sentences.interview: I wanted a challenging role.
sentences.business: He wanted to challenge the status quo.
sentences.formal: She wanted to challenge the decision legally.
sentences.informal: I wanted a real challenge.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a challenge' or 'wanted to challenge' — both correct. Very popular in interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: information given about performance or quality

sentences.daily: I wanted feedback on my work.
sentences.office: She wanted honest feedback from her manager.
sentences.interview: I always wanted constructive feedback.
sentences.business: He wanted feedback from the client.
sentences.formal: She wanted written feedback.
sentences.informal: I just wanted your feedback.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted feedback' is very professional and shows growth mindset.

words.simpleMeaning: a proposal or a deal presented for acceptance

sentences.daily: I wanted a better offer.
sentences.office: She wanted to accept the offer.
sentences.interview: I wanted a competitive offer.
sentences.business: He wanted to close the offer.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal offer letter.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a good offer.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted an offer' or 'wanted to offer' — both natural in job and business contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: a scheduled meeting or arrangement

sentences.daily: I wanted an appointment with the doctor.
sentences.office: She wanted to schedule an appointment.
sentences.interview: I wanted an appointment with the HR team.
sentences.business: He wanted an appointment to discuss the contract.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal appointment letter.
sentences.informal: I just wanted an appointment.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted an appointment' — very common in healthcare, office, and business contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: a situation that makes something possible

sentences.daily: I always wanted this kind of opportunity.
sentences.office: She wanted an opportunity to lead the project.
sentences.interview: I wanted this opportunity since college.
sentences.business: He wanted an opportunity in the market.
sentences.formal: She wanted the opportunity to present.
sentences.informal: I just wanted one good opportunity.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted an opportunity' — very powerful in interviews and goal-setting.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who shares in an activity or business

sentences.daily: I wanted a partner for the project.
sentences.office: She wanted a reliable business partner.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to work with a strong partner.
sentences.business: He wanted a funding partner.
sentences.formal: She wanted an official partnership.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a partner to work with.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a partner' — both personal and professional use.

words.simpleMeaning: a formal meeting to assess suitability

sentences.daily: I wanted the interview to go well.
sentences.office: She wanted to interview more candidates.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to get an interview here.
sentences.business: He wanted to interview the expert.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal interview process.
sentences.informal: I just wanted one interview.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to give/get an interview' — very natural in job-search discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: a person's professional life and progression

sentences.daily: I always wanted a successful career.
sentences.office: She wanted a career in management.
sentences.interview: I wanted a career that combined passion and purpose.
sentences.business: He wanted a career in entrepreneurship.
sentences.formal: She wanted a career in public policy.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a good career.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a career in...' is one of the most common interview sentence starters.

words.simpleMeaning: forward or onward movement toward a goal

sentences.daily: I always wanted to see progress in myself.
sentences.office: She wanted to track progress on the project.
sentences.interview: I wanted visible progress in my role.
sentences.business: He wanted progress reports weekly.
sentences.formal: She wanted documented progress.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to see some progress.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted progress' — very common in professional and self-improvement contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: something given for good work or behavior

sentences.daily: I wanted a reward for my hard work.
sentences.office: She wanted to reward her team.
sentences.interview: I always wanted a role with rewards for performance.
sentences.business: He wanted a reward program for loyal clients.
sentences.formal: She wanted formal recognition as a reward.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a reward.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a reward' — use in personal motivation, HR, and business contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: a period of rest and travel away from work

sentences.daily: I wanted a vacation after my exams.
sentences.office: She wanted a paid vacation.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to join a company that values vacation.
sentences.business: He wanted a vacation before the big product launch.
sentences.formal: She wanted formal leave for vacation.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a vacation!

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a vacation' is natural in daily life, work-life balance, and office discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: the movement of someone or something to a different place

sentences.daily: I wanted to transfer to a different city.
sentences.office: She wanted a transfer to the head office.
sentences.interview: I wanted a transfer for better growth.
sentences.business: He wanted to transfer the funds.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal transfer request.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a transfer.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a transfer' or 'wanted to transfer' — both common in office and banking.

words.simpleMeaning: a legal agreement between two parties

sentences.daily: I wanted to read the contract carefully.
sentences.office: She wanted to sign the contract.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to work under a fair contract.
sentences.business: He wanted a long-term contract.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal contract.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to see the contract.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a contract' or 'wanted to sign/read the contract' — very common in business.

words.simpleMeaning: the latest time by which something must be done

sentences.daily: I wanted to finish before the deadline.
sentences.office: She wanted to extend the deadline.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to be known for meeting deadlines.
sentences.business: He wanted a strict deadline for delivery.
sentences.formal: She wanted the deadline in writing.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to beat the deadline.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to meet the deadline' — very common in professional and academic settings.

words.simpleMeaning: a gathering of people to discuss something

sentences.daily: I wanted to skip the morning meeting.
sentences.office: She wanted to schedule a meeting.
sentences.interview: I wanted a quick introductory meeting.
sentences.business: He wanted a meeting with the investor.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal board meeting.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a quick meeting.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a meeting' or 'wanted to attend/skip the meeting' — very natural.

words.simpleMeaning: a written or spoken account of something

sentences.daily: I wanted to read the full report.
sentences.office: She wanted to submit the report by Monday.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to write detailed reports.
sentences.business: He wanted a financial report.
sentences.formal: She wanted the report in a standard format.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to check the report.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to submit/read the report' — very common in office and business settings.

words.simpleMeaning: an increase in salary; to lift or elevate

sentences.daily: I wanted a salary raise.
sentences.office: She wanted to raise the issue in the meeting.
sentences.interview: I always wanted a raise based on performance.
sentences.business: He wanted to raise funding for the startup.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal salary raise letter.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a raise!

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a raise' — very common in salary negotiation and performance review contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: an extra payment given for good work

sentences.daily: I wanted a bonus for my extra effort.
sentences.office: She wanted to give bonuses to the team.
sentences.interview: I always wanted a performance-based bonus.
sentences.business: He wanted a bonus clause in the contract.
sentences.formal: She wanted the bonus in writing.
sentences.informal: I just wanted that bonus!

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a bonus' — very common in employee motivation and salary discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: trust in someone or something

sentences.daily: I wanted my parents' confidence.
sentences.office: She wanted her manager's confidence in her work.
sentences.interview: I wanted to instil confidence in the interviewer.
sentences.business: He wanted the investor's confidence.
sentences.formal: She wanted formal confidence in the system.
sentences.informal: I just wanted your confidence in me.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted confidence' — used for both self-confidence and trust from others.

words.simpleMeaning: an experienced person who guides a less experienced one

sentences.daily: I always wanted a mentor in life.
sentences.office: She wanted to find a mentor at work.
sentences.interview: I always wanted a mentor in this field.
sentences.business: He wanted a business mentor.
sentences.formal: She wanted a formal mentorship program.
sentences.informal: I just wanted a good mentor.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted a mentor' — common in career, academic, and self-development discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to do something well

sentences.daily: I always wanted to develop new skills.
sentences.office: She wanted better technical skills.
sentences.interview: I always wanted skills that stood out.
sentences.business: He wanted skills in digital marketing.
sentences.formal: She wanted skill development programs.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to learn a skill.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to develop/learn a skill' — very common in educational and career contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: the aim or desired result of one's efforts

sentences.daily: I wanted to achieve my personal goal.
sentences.office: She wanted clear goals for the quarter.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to set and achieve goals.
sentences.business: He wanted business goals in writing.
sentences.formal: She wanted to document all goals.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to reach my goal.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to achieve a goal' — extremely common in interviews and self-development.

words.simpleMeaning: to express dissatisfaction about something

sentences.daily: I wanted to complain about the noise.
sentences.office: She wanted to complain to HR.
sentences.interview: I never wanted to complain — I looked for solutions.
sentences.business: He wanted to complain about the delay.
sentences.formal: She wanted to complain in writing.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to complain a little.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to complain' — contrasts nicely with 'wanted to solve'. Good for interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: to stop doing something or to resign from a job

sentences.daily: I wanted to quit bad habits.
sentences.office: She wanted to quit the toxic workplace.
sentences.interview: I never wanted to quit — I wanted to grow.
sentences.business: He wanted to quit the partnership.
sentences.formal: She wanted to quit and give formal notice.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to quit and rest.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to quit' — natural in job change and lifestyle discussions.

words.simpleMeaning: to make a formal request for something

sentences.daily: I wanted to apply for the scholarship.
sentences.office: She wanted to apply for the manager role.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to apply here.
sentences.business: He wanted to apply for a business loan.
sentences.formal: She wanted to apply through official channels.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to apply and see.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to apply' — very common in education, job search, and business.

words.simpleMeaning: to try to win something against others

sentences.daily: I always wanted to compete in sports.
sentences.office: She wanted to compete in the industry.
sentences.interview: I wanted to compete with the best.
sentences.business: He wanted to compete globally.
sentences.formal: She wanted to compete in the tender.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to compete and win.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to compete' — natural in sports, academic, and business contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to investigate or discover something new

sentences.daily: I always wanted to explore new places.
sentences.office: She wanted to explore new markets.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to explore diverse challenges.
sentences.business: He wanted to explore investment opportunities.
sentences.formal: She wanted to explore strategic options.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to explore a new hobby.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to explore' — excellent in interview, travel, and business contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to give something to a common purpose

sentences.daily: I always wanted to contribute to my community.
sentences.office: She wanted to contribute more to the team.
sentences.interview: I always wanted to contribute meaningfully.
sentences.business: He wanted to contribute to the startup's growth.
sentences.formal: She wanted to contribute to the research paper.
sentences.informal: I just wanted to contribute something.

words.usageNote: 'Wanted to contribute' — one of the strongest phrases in interviews and cover letters.
